feed.title: garden.errbufferoverfl.me - home
feed.description: An open collection of notes, resources, sketches, and explorations I'm cultivating.
feed.subtitle: Recent content in home on
feed.author: errbuffoverfl
feed.rights: This content is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International unless otherwise specified.

entry.title: 17 Forbidden Places You Can't Visit
entry.summary: From an island infested with snakes to a vault holding a very special secret recipe, these dangerous, mysterious, or otherwise forbidden places are off-limits to the public.

entry.title: 5 types of threat – how those who want to divide us use language to stoke violence
entry.summary: Events like the riots in Brazil, the two years before it and the mass shooting at the Colorado LGBTQ nightclub each occurred after certain groups repeatedly directed dangerous rhetoric against others.

entry.title: A Massachusetts bill could allow prisoners to swap their organs for their freedom
entry.summary: This article is from The Checkup, MIT Technology Review’s weekly biotech newsletter. To receive it in your inbox every Thursday, sign up here.

entry.title: A Racially Biased Scoring System Helps Pick Who Receives Housing in L.A. – The Markup
entry.summary: So, no scoring data has been released? On a potentially racially biased system? Challenge accepted. Colin wanted that data. Link

entry.title: A small mistake does not a complex systems failure make
entry.summary: The source of the problem was reportedly a single engineer who made a small mistake with a file transfer. Here’s what I’d like you to ponder, dear reader.

entry.title: Andrew Tate: research has long shown how feminist progress is always followed by a misogynistic backlash
entry.summary: Andrew Tate is a man who has made a living out of controversy. The extensive media coverage garnered since his arrest in Romania on suspicion of rape, human trafficking and organised crime has largely called him out for his misogynistic views.

entry.title: April King — Refresh vs. Long-lived Access Tokens
entry.summary: Refresh vs. Long-lived Access Tokens One question which I frequently receive is: Why would you want to use long-lived refresh tokens that generate short-lived access tokens as commonly seen in OAuth 2.

entry.title: Architects, Anti-Patterns, and Organizational Fuckery
entry.summary: I recently wrote a twitter thread on the proper role of architects, or as I put it, tongue-in-cheek-ily, whether or not architect is a “bullshit role”.

entry.title: Australia’s twice extended deadline for torture prevention is today, but we’ve missed it again
entry.summary: Today marks the (already twice extended) deadline for Australia to meet its international obligation to implement torture prevention bodies. The role of these bodies is to monitor treatment and conditions in places where people are deprived of their liberty, like prisons and detention centres.

entry.title: Can reading help heal us and process our emotions – or is that just a story we tell ourselves?
entry.summary: The oldest known library, dating back to the second millennium BC, in Thebes, Egypt, reportedly bore a sign above its portals in Greek: Psyches Iatreion, translated as “healing place of the soul”.

entry.title: Civilization States Are Profoundly Illiberal
entry.summary: The economic and technological convergence of globalization did not lead to a singular cosmopolitan order, but to a great divergence, in which prospering emergent nations, most notably China, once again attained the wherewithal to chart a path forward based on their own civilizational foundations.

entry.title: Climate change trauma has real impacts on cognition and the brain, wildfire survivors study shows
entry.summary: The Research Brief is a short take about interesting academic work. Psychological trauma from extreme weather and climate events, such as wildfires, can have long-term impacts on survivors’ brains and cognitive functioning, especially how they process distractions, my team’s new research shows.

entry.title: Consensus decision-making is surprisingly effective in both communities and workplaces
entry.summary: If you’re in a leadership position — at work or in the community — you make decisions and oversee decision-making processes.

entry.title: Councils against data discrimination
entry.summary: Data discrimination occurs when individuals or groups are treated unfairly because of characteristics or traits identified through the collection and analysis of their data.

entry.title: CYBER: Iran’s AI-Powered Surveillance State
entry.summary: On January 31, a court in Iran handed out a combined sentence of 10 years to a couple who danced outside of Azadi Tower in Tehran, Iran.

entry.title: CYBER: The Nextdoor Poster to Political Activist Pipeline
entry.summary: We’ve all heard about how Facebook is destroying democracy. How Twitter enables the loudest, dumbest voices to have the most influence.

entry.title: CYBER: What Was Havana Syndrome, the Mystery Illness that Hit American Spies?
entry.summary: In 2016, Americans working in Cuba began to experience something strange. Something that is, to this day, unexplained. They felt a pressure in the brain, a ringing in their ear, and in the aftermath … a distressing sense of fatigue.

entry.title: Dante was the founder of Italy’s right-wing, claims culture minister – an expert explains why he’s wrong
entry.summary: “Dante was the founder of right-wing thinking in our country”. So proclaimed Italy’s culture minister, Gennaro Sangiuliano, at an electoral meeting of the prime minister Giorgia Meloni’s party, Fratelli d’Italia, in January.

entry.title: Data-hungry corporations should clean their own messes — instead of asking us to
entry.summary: The ‘right to be forgotten’ isn’t enough to protect our privacy. The onus must be placed on the organisations harvesting information.

entry.title: Democracies don’t just bounce back after dictatorships – Argentina’s Oscar contender shows what justice afterward looks like
entry.summary: When the director and the star of “Argentina, 1985” stepped on stage to accept a 2023 Golden Globe Award, the title of the film may not have meant much to many Americans in the audience.

entry.title: Democratization of cybersecurity
entry.summary: Avrohom Gottheil, founder of #AskTheCEO Media, recaps the fascinating conversation he had with Vishal Salvi, SVP & CISO at Infosys, about the evolution of cybersecurity and the question about whose responsibility it is to keep us safe.

entry.title: Design thinking was supposed to fix the world. Where did it go wrong?
entry.summary: When Kyle Cornforth first walked into IDEO’s San Francisco offices in 2011, she felt she had entered a whole new world.

entry.title: Disabled people were Holocaust victims, too: they were excluded from German society and murdered by Nazi programs
entry.summary: When Dominic Perrottet admitted to wearing a Nazi uniform to his 21st birthday party, he apologised to Jews and veterans – but not to the other groups who were persecuted by the Nazis, including disabled people.

entry.title: Don’t kill the curl grubs in your garden – they could be native beetle babies
entry.summary: Have you ever been in the garden and found a large, white, C-shaped grub with a distinctive brown head and six legs clustered near the head?

entry.title: Exploding carp numbers are ‘like a house of horrors’ for our rivers. Is it time to unleash carp herpes?
entry.summary: With widespread La Niña flooding in the Murray-Darling Basin, common carp (Cyprinus carpio) populations are having a boom year. Videos of writhing masses of both adult and young fish illustrate that all is not well in our rivers.

entry.title: Exxon scientists accurately forecast climate change back in the 1970s – what if we had listened to them and acted then?
entry.summary: Writers of speculative- and science-fiction often identify a key point in time and explore how a seemingly insignificant event might change the path of humanity.

entry.title: Feature | An almanac of immeasurable things
entry.summary: If you were in the Western Hemisphere, and you walked into a room full of strangers talking about Katrina, Harvey, Mitchell or Maria, you might know who they were talking about.

entry.title: Four possible consequences of El Niño returning in 2023
entry.summary: Every two to seven years, the equatorial Pacific Ocean gets up to 3°C warmer (what we know as an El Niño event) or colder (La Niña) than usual, triggering a cascade of effects felt around the world.

entry.title: Google didn’t break your brain : a history of distraction
entry.summary: If you suspect that 21st-century technology has broken your brain, it will be reassuring to know that attention spans have never been what they used to be.

entry.title: Hidden loopholes and privacy risks loom over online age check laws
entry.summary: What’s the context? Louisiana joins Europe and the UK in passing age verification laws to boost minors’ online safety, but experts warn of the dangers From Louisiana to UK, lawmakers back age verification Governments say measures needed to protect children Rights groups sound alarm over privacy, s

entry.title: Holocaust remembrance: we must beware of well-intentioned mythmaking as events pass out of living memory
entry.summary: In ancient Rome, the saeculum, or “the age”, was the span of living memory, the telling of an event passed down from the oldest to the youngest.

entry.title: How Big Tech Rewrote the Nation’s First Cellphone Repair Law – The Markup
entry.summary: This story was published in partnership with Grist, a nonprofit media organization covering climate, justice, and solutions. You can subscribe to its weekly newsletter here.

entry.title: How consecutive Conservative governments destroyed union rights – a timeline of the UK’s anti-strike laws since the 1970s
entry.summary: Rishi Sunak’s government is attempting to introduce tough new laws that would further limit the right to take industrial action amid continuing strikes in the UK.

entry.title: How does methadone work as a heroin-replacement therapy? And what about the longer-acting buprenorphine?
entry.summary: Around 1% of Australian adults have tried heroin in their lifetime and 2.7% have used pharmaceutical opioids for non-medical purposes in the past 12 months.

entry.title: How Roomba tester’s private images ended up on Facebook
entry.summary: A Roomba recorded a woman on the toilet. How did screenshots end up on social media? This episode we go behind the scenes of an MIT Technology Review investigation that uncovered how sensitive photos taken by an AI powered vacuum were leaked and landed on the internet.

entry.title: How Telegram groups can be used by police to find protesters
entry.summary: China Report is MIT Technology Review’s newsletter about technology developments in China. Sign up to receive it in your inbox every Tuesday.

entry.title: How the distortion of Martin Luther King Jr.‘s words enables more, not less, racial division within American society
entry.summary: U.S. Rep. Chip Roy of Texas is just the latest conservative lawmaker to misuse the words of the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr.

entry.title: How to Keep a Garden Journal to Make You a Better Gardener
entry.summary: Journaling is one of the most rewarding hobbies. Each journal is a beautiful and unique way to record your thoughts, plan your dreams, or track cyclical events.

entry.title: If not vegan, then what? An investigation of three options
entry.summary: Suppose a person is very concerned about the ethical issues around food and farming, especially animal welfare, but for whatever reason finds that a wholly plant-based diet does not work for them.

entry.title: If you haven’t joined a union, it’s time you paid to benefit from union deals
entry.summary: A long overdue public debate has started in Australia about “free riding” in industrial relations – when non-union members benefit from collective agreements negotiated by union members without contributing (through membership dues or other payments) to their negotiation and administration.

entry.title: Inside a US Neo-Nazi Homeschool Network With Thousands of Members
entry.summary: An Ohio couple has been unmasked as leaders of the neo-Nazi “Dissident Homeschool” Telegram channel that distributes lesson plans to 2,400 members.

entry.title: International Conspiracy of Mad Scientists
entry.summary: Welcome to the International Conspiracy of Mad Scientists, a lair for plotting and developing inventions to destroy authoritarianism. (“International Conspiracy of Mad Scientists” = ICMS = “I see a mess”!

entry.title: Jewish doctors in the Warsaw Ghetto secretly documented the effects of Nazi-imposed starvation, and the knowledge is helping researchers today – Podcast
entry.summary: During the years of suffering and tragedy that defined the Warsaw Ghetto in the midst of World War II, a team of Jewish doctors secretly documented the effects of starvation on the human body when the Nazis severely limited the amount of food available in the Jewish ghetto.

entry.title: Legal but not in reach: The state of abortion in Australia
entry.summary: Australia’s position as America-lite, a little sibling stumbling along the line between voracious neoliberalism and violent abnegation of its own history, comes into distinct relief every so often.

entry.title: Listening Note Taking Strategies
entry.summary: To take notes effectively, you need to have a note-taking system that works for you. Having well organised notes will make them easier to understand and study from.

entry.title: More means less: Extended copyright benefits the corporate few, not the public
entry.summary: Who benefits from Canada’s recent extension of copyright? Dead authors? Students? Marginalized writers? If you answered no to all of these, you’d be correct.

entry.title: On class as a product of struggle
entry.summary: As climate-induced floods hit both sides of the Tasman with grim frequency, an article about class feels almost frivolous. Yet to separate climate from class is to create a false binary.

entry.title: On Gentle Violence
entry.summary: In anarchist circles, we often hear about the subject of violence in its direct forms, such as the violence in state repression, violence enacted by the police, violence perpetrated by individuals with a different ideological alignment.

entry.title: OpenAI Used Kenyan Workers Making $2 an Hour to Filter Traumatic Content from ChatGPT
entry.summary: The workers were tasked to label and filter out toxic data from ChatGPT’s training dataset and were forced to read graphic details of NSFW content such as child sexual abuse, bestiality, murder, suicide, torture, self-harm, and incest, Time reported.

entry.title: ORG welcomes Apple’s move to expand encryption
entry.summary: Open Rights Group welcomes Apple’s move to improve security with its Advanced Data Protection feature for iCloud. The feature allows additional sensitive files, such as photos and notes, to be encrypted in the cloud.

entry.title: Parsing which foods are healthy and which are less so isn’t always straightforward – a new rating system aims to demystify the process
entry.summary: Many people aim to start the year off with healthier food choices. Link

entry.title: People with disabilities in group homes are suffering shocking abuse. New housing models could prevent harm
entry.summary: The National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) was designed to be a market-based system that would shift power from government and providers to consumers.

entry.title: Proton’s Raffle: Helping RDR Keep Big Tech Accountable Globally in 2023
entry.summary: The 2022 Big Tech Scorecard evaluated and ranked 14 of the world’s most powerful digital platforms on their disclosed policies and practices affecting user’s right to freedom of expression and information and privacy.

entry.title: RDR Raises Concerns About UNESCO’s Proposed Framework for Regulating Digital Platforms
entry.summary: The 2022 Big Tech Scorecard evaluated and ranked 14 of the world’s most powerful digital platforms on their disclosed policies and practices affecting user’s right to freedom of expression and information and privacy.

entry.title: Regina Twala was a towering intellectual and activist in Eswatini - but she was erased from history
entry.summary: Born in South Africa and exiled to neighbouring Eswatini, Regina Twala was one of southern Africa’s most important intellectuals: a pioneering writer, academic, political activist and feminist.

entry.title: Reviled, reclaimed and respected: the history of the word ‘queer’
entry.summary: Recently, a number of people have questioned or critiqued the use of the word “queer” to describe LGBTIQA+ folk. One writer to the Guardian claimed that the “q-word” was as derogatory and offensive as the “n-word”, and should not be used.

entry.title: Scientists Determine Best Place to Stand In Your House When a Nuke Hits
entry.summary: Nuclear bombs are the most devastating weapon on the planet. The blast from the explosion generated by Russia’s “Satan II” nuclear missile would destroy all of Central Park.

entry.title: Social Ecology & Solarpunk: The aesthetics of radical change
entry.summary: This article is a response to the first week of a course put together by the Institute for Social Ecology and Solarpunk Surf Club called “Solarpunk, Art, and Social Ecological Aesthetics”.

entry.title: Stop feeding the hype and start resisting
entry.summary: Three weeks ago, I wrote a blogpost about how ChatGPT is a “stochastic parrot” (a term coined by Bender, Gebru, McMillan-Major, & Shmitchell, 2021; see also this video for an explanation) and when used for academic (and other) writing constitutes automated plagiarism.

entry.title: That Garbage Could Be a Garden!
entry.summary: Antinote: Minneapolis-based mutual aid group Twin Cities Logistics Collective dropped a fun instructional pamphlet this month, hoping to inspire and assist home and community gardeners in Minnesota and elsewhere as they begin to think beyond the winter darkness towards a spring of abundant sowing.

entry.title: The Alarming Rise of Dangerous Speech – The Markup
entry.summary: Hello, friends, This week, new details emerged about how social media platforms allowed violent rhetoric to circulate freely on their platforms in the weeks leading up to the Jan.

entry.title: The Chatbot Problem
entry.summary: In 2020, a chatbot named Replika advised the Italian journalist Candida Morvillo to commit murder. “There is one who hates artificial intelligence.

entry.title: The Cloud Is a Prison. Can the Local-First Software Movement Set Us Free?
entry.summary: A few years ago, the discussion forum Hacker News, where engineers collectively decide what other engineers should read, developed a quirk.

entry.title: The Documentary World’s Identity Crisis
entry.summary: This article was featured in One Great Story, New York’s reading recommendation newsletter. Sign up here to get it nightly.

entry.title: The Download: untrustworthy AI, and Rust’s origin story
entry.summary: This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology.

entry.title: The evidence is clear : learning styles theory doesn’t work
entry.summary: Picture two English classrooms. In the first, the teacher is teaching Macbeth to a group of eager students, and has planned the lesson meticulously, taking into account their individual learning differences, with the children sitting in three groups.

entry.title: The Holocaust: remembering the powerful acts of ‘ordinary people’
entry.summary: “The past is a foreign country; they do things differently there,” the British author LP Hartley once wrote, hinting at the mystique of history – the idea that people in the past were somehow different to us in the 21st century.

entry.title: The Housewives of White Supremacy
entry.summary: In a podcast interview posted last spring, Nicole Jorgenson, a singer and former schoolteacher in North Dakota, explains to her host that she has never been happier since marrying and having children.

entry.title: The Perfect Victim
entry.summary: Samuel Little says he killed 93 people, preying on women from the margins of society. Again and again, police across the country failed to stop him.

entry.title: The Secret Life of the 500+ Cables That Run the Internet
entry.summary: The concert is in London. You’re watching it live from your home in Atlanta. Link

entry.title: The Soul of Kazakhstan
entry.summary: Only last year, violent unrest broke out across Kazakhstan following popular anger over rising gas prices. According to the official figures, this brief and bloody uprising resulted in 227 deaths.

entry.title: The state of responsible technology
entry.summary: Consideration of how to deploy technology responsibly has become critical as tech and data have become more entrenched in modern society and business operations.

entry.title: The Supreme Court may overhaul how you live online
entry.summary: This article is from The Technocrat, MIT Technology Review’s weekly tech policy newsletter about power, politics, and Silicon Valley. To receive it in your inbox every Friday, sign up here.

entry.title: The World’s Farms Are Hooked on Phosphorus. It’s a Problem
entry.summary: Disrupting Earth’s chemical cycles brings trouble. But planet-warming carbon dioxide isn’t the only element whose cycle we’ve turned wonky—we’ve got a phosphorus problem too.

entry.title: This Algorithm Could Ruin Your Life
entry.summary: It was October 2021, and Imane, a 44-year-old mother of three, was still in pain from the abdominal surgery she had undergone a few weeks earlier.

entry.title: Tiny Free Stores & Libraries of Everything: Steps Towards a Post-Capitalist Future
entry.summary: I made a tiny free store to share resources with my fellow neighbors and give things away for free that otherwise would have ended up in a landfill or thrift store.

entry.title: TLDR: We need a united class not a united left
entry.summary: In the Twitter era, references to articles are often met with the response “TLDR”, which means Too Long, Didn’t Read, can you summarize it?

entry.title: USENIX Security '22 - Experimental Security Analysis of the App Model in Business Collaboration
entry.summary: USENIX Security ‘22 - Experimental Security Analysis of the App Model in Business Collaboration Platforms Yunang Chen, Yue Gao, Nick Ceccio, Rahul Chatterjee, Kassem Fawaz, and Earlence Fernandes, University of Wisconsin–Madison Business Collaboration Platforms like Microsoft Teams and Slack ena

entry.title: Veganism: why we should see it as a political movement rather than a dietary choice
entry.summary: The start of a new year these days signals the launch of Veganuary, a campaign which encourages people to give up animal products for the month of January.

entry.title: What Writers and Editors Are Reading: January 2023
entry.summary: Wish you could ask your favorite writers what they’re reading? We did it for you in our January round-up of articles recommended by writers and editors we love.

entry.title: Why we should all learn to love stinging nettles
entry.summary: Thinking of stinging nettles (Urtica dioica) may bring to mind childhood memories of legs burning as you whizzed down country lanes on your bike.

entry.title: Why your recycling doesn't always get recycled
entry.summary: A plastic bag, a dirty pizza box, plastic utensils, paper napkins, and a soda can—a single takeout meal can feel like a game of recycling trivia.

entry.title: Words Mean Things Mutual Aid
entry.summary: Ever since the start of the pandemic I’ve seen a lot of projects pop up that claim to be mutual aid projects.

entry.title: ‘Everything you’ve been told is a lie!’ Inside the wellness-to-fascism pipeline
entry.summary: Jane – not her real name – is nervous about speaking to me. She has asked that I don’t identify her or the small, south-coast Devon town in which she lives.

entry.title: ‘No home to go to, and no means of living’: how colonial vagrancy laws punished the poor
entry.summary: Vagrancy – being found in the street without any visible means of support – was a crime in many parts of Australia right up to the final decades of the 20th century.

entry.title: ‘Whisper networks’ thrive when women lose faith in formal systems of reporting sexual harassment
entry.summary: The Research Brief is a short take about interesting academic work. Whisper networks – informal channels that women use to warn others about sexual harassment, abuse or assault – take root because formal reporting systems can re-traumatize people who have been harmed.

entry.title: A dangerous pesticide isn’t being monitored in key bird of prey populations - we’re shedding light on that gap
entry.summary: It was once regarded as a miracle chemical to protect against disease and improve global food production. The man who discovered its properties even won a Nobel Prize for medicine.

entry.title: A story of legends, families and capitalism: a candid history of the Christmas tree
entry.summary: The Christmas tree is a modern invention. It is a largely secular symbol, having no basis in the Bible. There are many trees in the Bible, from the Tree of Knowledge and the Tree of Life in Genesis to the reference to Christ’s cross as a “tree” in Acts.

entry.title: Abolition, Ecology, and Afrofuturism Some Thoughts
entry.summary: For several decades, many Black activists and others have advanced an intensified focus during August on the struggle of our incarcerated family.

entry.title: Adjusting the intensity of farming can help address climate change
entry.summary: We have little chance of tackling climate change and reducing biodiversity loss without a redesign of the world’s largest industry: agriculture and food.

entry.title: Against a Liberal Abolitionism
entry.summary: In the explosion of interest in the topic of abolitionism during and after the explosive summer of 2020 its meaning and purpose has become distorted in its trek through the popular imagination.

entry.title: Against Self-Work A critique of care as a form of labor
entry.summary: Life as a Form of Work “It is worth asking, do we presume to attack the mega-structures of State and Capital without changing the relations between ourselves and the tasks that go along with this — questioning the dominant concepts of time and rhythm, of survival and life, of symbol and reality?

entry.title: Anarchism and Cryptocurrency
entry.summary: Cryptocurrency, a digital currency in which transactions are verified and recorded by a decentralized system using cryptography, rather than by a centralized authority, is a controversial technology amongst anarchists, even though it is often used as a tool for undermining state power.

entry.title: Anti-Racism and Anti-colonialism An Open Letter to my Black Kin
entry.summary: Image description — Black and white photo with a fist raised in the Black Power salute. Link

entry.title: Bad fire science can kill our threatened species. It’s time to cooperate with nature
entry.summary: For four years, naturalist Allison Dixon regularly walked from dusk until dawn at the Warrungup Spring bush reserve south of Perth, carefully documenting every western ringtail possum she saw.

entry.title: Betrayal: A Critical Analysis of Rape Culture in Anarchist Subcultures
entry.summary: A reprint of a zine from the US written in 2013 about the specific ways anarhist subcultures recreate and perpetuate rape culture.

entry.title: Canadian engineers call for change to their private ‘iron ring’ ceremony steeped in colonialism
entry.summary: For almost a century, iron rings worn by Canadian engineers have been shrouded in mystery — at least for outsiders like me, an English professor and director of the Critical Media Lab at University of Waterloo, who teaches ethics to engineers.

entry.title: Competitive Nation-Building
entry.summary: The backlash against decades of hyper-globalization, which spread wealth to new winners across the emerging economies while hollowing out the manufacturing base of more advanced countries, most notably the United States, is exhausting its first wave of reactive populism and entering the reconstructi

entry.title: Comprehensive Rust 🦀
entry.summary: This is a free Rust course developed by the Android team at Google. The course covers the full spectrum of Rust, from basic syntax to advanced topics like generics and error handling.

entry.title: Dark ecotourism at Toolangi: the invisible destruction of a forest
entry.summary: A squiggle of roads wind through the Toolangi Forest, roughly an hour out of Melbourne. The short distance between the roads renders the entirety of the forest readily accessible by truck, bisecting the land into a tessellation of prospective and present logging coupes.

entry.title: Flour Trip: (Almost) Everything You’ve Ever Wanted to Know About Flour
entry.summary: One woman’s journey into the heart of grain and how our flour is made. Link

entry.title: Food shortages: five ways to fix ‘unfair’ supply chains
entry.summary: UK food prices soared by more than 16% in 2022 as record inflation pushed up the prices of everything from bread to beans.

entry.title: From Women’s Well-Being to Radiation-Contaminated Waste to English Football – The Markup
entry.summary: Data Is Plural is a weekly newsletter of useful/curious datasets. This edition, dated , has been republished with permission of the author.

entry.title: Genocides persist, nearly 70 years after the Holocaust – but there are recognized ways to help prevent them
entry.summary: The newly formed United Nations passed its first international treaty on Dec. 9, 1948, just three years after the Holocaust ended.

entry.title: Grassroots AIDS activists fought for and won affordable HIV treatments around the world – but PEPFAR didn’t change governments and pharma
entry.summary: The President’s Emergency Program for AIDS Relief, or PEPFAR, has revolutionized the fight against global AIDS over the last 20 years.

entry.title: How To Ferment Low-Sodium Sauerkraut
entry.summary: Wash the cabbage and remove the outer leaves. Finely chop or grate the cabbage into a large bowl. Salt is optional with this particular kraut, so feel free to skip it, or add as much as you want.

entry.title: In defence of woodlice and their complicated sex lives
entry.summary: Lots of adults dislike woodlice. Some are physically revolted by them. But this distaste is cultural rather than innate, since most small children are well-disposed towards woodlice and happy to handle them.

entry.title: It’s not all about gender or ethnicity: a blind spot in diversity programs is holding equality back
entry.summary: Diversity, inclusion and equity policies are now broadly endorsed in Australian organisations. But not all diversities are equal. Our research suggests while programs for women and some racial minorities are being embraced, other diversities are excluded.

entry.title: Janaka Malwatta’s blackbirds don’t mate with starlings
entry.summary: Racism is the destruction of human relations and undoes the bonds of life. Link

entry.title: Modern slavery: UK’s focus on ‘genuine’ victims has failed survivors since the 1800s
entry.summary: The exploitation of tens of thousands of people through modern slavery is a human rights crisis. There are an estimated 130,000 people currently living in modern slavery in the UK alone.

entry.title: Over the holidays, try talking to your relatives like an anthropologist
entry.summary: How is it possible to spend so much time with your parents and grandparents and not really know them? This question has puzzled me as an anthropologist.

entry.title: Palm kernel product imported for use on dairy farms may actually be harmful to cows
entry.summary: Each year, New Zealand imports about 2 million tonnes of palm kernel expeller (PKE), a by-product of palm-oil processing in Indonesia and Malaysia, to feed dairy cows, at a cost of NZ$800 million.

entry.title: Porn, Piracy, Fraud: What Lurks Inside Google’s Black Box Ad Empire
entry.summary: In late 2021, the right-wing site Conservative Beaver published a story falsely claiming the FBI had arrested Pfizer’s CEO for fraud.

entry.title: Producing Transdermal Estrogen: A Do-It-Yourself Guide
entry.summary: This guide describes how a small collective produced and distributed transdermal estrogen using reproducible do-it-yourself methods. What follows here is not medical advice; it is a report on an experiment in process, providing proof of concept.

entry.title: Rural Americans aren’t included in inflation figures – and for them, the cost of living may be rising faster
entry.summary: When the Federal Reserve convenes at the end of January 2023 to set interest rates, it will be guided by one key bit of data: the U.

entry.title: Schrodinger’s Whore; Sex Work and Academia
entry.summary: The first time I noticed the difference between myself and my peers was receiving a client email while studying at the university library, tilting my phone screen to ensure no wandering eyes would happen upon the deviant contents.

entry.title: The Cake Recipe That Was a Secret for Two Decades
entry.summary: A century ago, chiffon cake did not exist. It did not grace the plates of our forebears, surreal in its fluffiness, with its thousand tiny holes, more air than sugar.

entry.title: The Hidden Link Between Workaholism and Mental Health
entry.summary: Long hours on the job can temporarily ease the symptoms of depression and anxiety. But you’re better off leaving the office and facing your feelings head-on.

entry.title: The Left Cannot Just Denounce Cults/Abuse; We Must Undermine Its Conditions of Possibility
entry.summary: I will not be naming names in this piece but if you know, you know. My love and solidarity to all movement survivors.

entry.title: The lie of aqua nullius, ‘nobody’s water’, prevails in Australia. Indigenous water reserves are not enough to deliver justice
entry.summary: When the British colonised Australia they assumed terra nullius, “nobody’s land”, and aqua nullius, “nobody’s water”. In 1992, terra nullius was overturned – but aqua nullius remains.

entry.title: The real reason why there’s a global rise in trans youth
entry.summary: I owe an apology to anyone who knew me between the years of 2016 and 2018, when I somehow found a way to derail every conversation into rambling about how bad it was about to get for queer and trans people across the globe.

entry.title: The trouble with tradwives
entry.summary: ‘Now tell me what you do for a living’, the Tik Tok begins. It’s from by @justpearlythings, aka Pearl Davis, and is entitled How to marry a high-value man and become a housewife.

entry.title: The weaponization of the federal government has a long history
entry.summary: Now that House Republicans have created a “Select Subcommittee on Weaponization of the Federal Government,” let’s revisit a classic of that power-abusing genre, featuring its greatest star, Richard M.

entry.title: This one-page calendar will change how you view the year
entry.summary: Each year, most of us throw out our old calendar and replace it with a new one. Link

entry.title: Threatening tech bosses with prison damages freedom of expression
entry.summary: Threatening people who provide online services with prison could result in more censorship of lawful posts. The Online Safety Bill as it stands damages our freedom of expression.

entry.title: Top 10 Most Read Pieces from 2022
entry.summary: Combing across 19th-century shores, seaweed collectors would wander for hours, tucking specimens into pouches and jars, before pasting their finds into artful albums.

entry.title: Transphobia is a Respectability Politic Thoughts on Black Community Defense
entry.summary: Im a Wild Thing Man caint house me. Too wild, to fierce, too bold— caint house me! Link

entry.title: Underpaid at home, vulnerable abroad: how seasonal job schemes are draining Pacific nations of vital workers
entry.summary: The economic impact of COVID on Pacific Island states, combined with major labour shortages in Australia and New Zealand, has created a perfect storm.

entry.title: We come to bury ChatGPT, not to praise it.
entry.summary: Large language models (LLMs) like the GPT family learn the statistical structure of language by optimising their ability to predict missing words in sentences (as in ‘The cat sat on the [BLANK]’).

entry.title: We live in a time of ‘late capitalism’. But what does that mean? And what’s so late about it?
entry.summary: The term “late capitalism” seems to be everywhere as a trending meme – often used as a kind of shorthand to illustrate the absurdities of certain free market economies.

entry.title: What is Cop City?
entry.summary: The Atlanta Police Foundation is building a police militarization facility for police to train in urban warfare. “To be clear — cop city is not just a controversial training center.

entry.title: ‘Discriminatory impact on First Nations people’: coroner calls for urgent bail reform in Veronica Nelson inquest
entry.summary: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised this article contains names and/or images of deceased people. Nelson was suffering from an undiagnosed medical condition when she was arrested on suspicion of shoplifting and alleged breach of bail in January 2020.

entry.title: All My Takeout Has Delivered a Mountain of Trash. So I Asked Experts How to Minimize It.
entry.summary: The pandemic has caused a spike in restaurant takeout containers — and the environmental damage that comes with them.

entry.title: Artificial intelligence is creating a new colonial world order
entry.summary: This story is the introduction to MIT Technology Review’s series on AI colonialism, which was supported by the MIT Knight Science Journalism Fellowship Program and the Pulitzer Center.

entry.title: As QAnon Falters, European Followers Flock to a Financial Conspiracy
entry.summary: Imagine you’re a jaded stalwart of the QAnon conspiracy. The latest batch of ‘drops’ – the cryptic posts into which you’ve read so much – aren’t quite as enticing, and you’re not even sure that Donald Trump will return to the Oval Office.

entry.title: Black History Is World History
entry.summary: View Original Link

entry.title: Chokepoint Capitalism: why we’ll all lose unless we stop Amazon, Spotify and other platforms squeezing cash from creators
entry.summary: In 2020, the independent authors and small publishers whose audiobooks reach their readers via Audible’s ACX platform smelled a rat.

entry.title: CSS Hell
entry.summary: Collection of common CSS mistakes, and how to fix them Link

entry.title: Digital privacy and security measures for staying safe while protesting
entry.summary: Digital privacy and security measures for staying safe while protesting may feel like a confusing, moving target. Link

entry.title: Failure to Cope 'Under Capitalism'
entry.summary: We have generational trauma. We are living through a global pandemic. We are literally neurodivergent and a minor. We are riddled with climate grief.

entry.title: Far from being profoundly destructive, we humans have deep capacities for sharing resources with generosity and foresight
entry.summary: In December 1968, the ecologist and biologist Garrett Hardin had an essay published in the journal Science called ‘The Tragedy of the Commons’.

entry.title: Finding the Unexpected Wonder in More Than 22,000 International Standards
entry.summary: From brewing tea to making shipping containers. Link

entry.title: Five ways you can help stop biodiversity loss in your area – and around the world
entry.summary: Biodiversity refers to the variety of life found on Earth and underpins the natural systems which grow our food, cleanse our air and water and regulate our climate.

entry.title: Have We Forgotten How to Read Critically?
entry.summary: This article was made possible because of the generous support of DAME members. During our Spring Member Drive, we urgently need your help to keep publishing.

entry.title: Helping male victims of domestic abuse can benefit society as a whole
entry.summary: Every year in early December, the UN holds its 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence. The issue is one that can affect everyone regardless of their sex, gender or gender identity.

entry.title: Henry Lee Lucas Was Considered America’s Most Prolific Serial Killer. But He Was Really a Serial Liar.
entry.summary: ‘The Confession Killer’ examines the true story of the man who said he killed nearly 600 people. Link

entry.title: How to Get Things Done When You Don’t Want to Do Anything
entry.summary: This April, I was feeling good. I’d figured out the public pool’s lane-reservation system and swam several times a week.

entry.title: How to Remember What You Read
entry.summary: It happens all the time. You read an amazing book, one so packed with wisdom that you think it’s going to change your life forever.

entry.title: How to study effectively
entry.summary: As noted above, it’s better to distribute the time you have available to study over a greater number of shorter sessions than it is to cram your studying into a single marathon session.

entry.title: How to write a better technical design document
entry.summary: It can feel daunting to write a technical spec. It can feel like too much hard work, or worse, busy work that’s not really important.

entry.title: Inside the World of Investigators Who Know You’ve Faked Your Death
entry.summary: ‘Faking your own death is a full-time job, and I can guarantee you that if you slip up with even the slightest sign of life, we will find you’.

entry.title: It started as a fringe philosophical theory about humanity’s future. It’s now richly funded and increasingly dangerous
entry.summary: There seems to be a growing recognition that humanity might be approaching the ‘end times’. Dire predictions of catastrophe clutter the news.

entry.title: On Soupgate and the limits of spectacle-based activism
entry.summary: In 1914, suffragette Mary Richardson of the Women’s Social and Political Union took a meat cleaver into London’s National Gallery and hacked six times at Velázquez’s The Toilet of Venus.

entry.title: Pro-choice crowdfunding has surged in the U.S. — but donating that way has risks
entry.summary: The Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization decision by the United States Supreme Court earlier this year overturned constitutional protections of reproductive choice for Americans.

entry.title: Re-Reading Is Inefficient. Here Are 8 Tips for Studying Smarter.
entry.summary: The memory researcher’s guide to studying. Link

entry.title: Routine Maintenance, by Meghan O’Gieblyn
entry.summary: Of all the attempts to pinpoint the origin of modernity—an exercise of which modernity never tires—my favorite begins with medieval monks.

entry.title: The Disappearing Art Of Maintenance
entry.summary: The R32 trains, nicknamed the Brightliners for their shiny unpainted exteriors, were built to last 35 years. (Imagine that: Your lifespan stamped into metal, your death prefigured.

entry.title: The Everyday, Eco-Friendly Actions That Save You Money at Home
entry.summary: Link

entry.title: The Great (Fake) Child-Sex-Trafficking Epidemic
entry.summary: A poster in the window of Cahoots Corner Cafe—great potatoes, good coffee—advertised a family event at the Oakdale, California, rodeo grounds.

entry.title: The lie of “expired” food and the disastrous truth of America’s food waste problem
entry.summary: Stop throwing your food away. Maybe you know the routine. Every so often, I go through my refrigerator, check labels on the items, and throw out anything that’s a month, or a week, or maybe a few days past the date on the label.

entry.title: The Real Cost of Starting a Homestead
entry.summary: We’ve all seen those fantastic documentaries about people who decided to live off-grid and pursue a life in nature. It’s easy to get swept up in the Instagram posts, hoping it’s as easy as taking the photos make it look.

entry.title: The Reluctant Prophet of Effective Altruism
entry.summary: The philosopher William MacAskill credits his personal transfiguration to an undergraduate seminar at Cambridge. Link

entry.title: The Things You Are Getting Wrong About White Supremacists Is What Allows Them To Grow
entry.summary: Twelve years ago, I packed up a Uhaul and left the home my son was born in. I drove across the country with him in a car seat, singing hours of nursery rhymes to keep him entertained.

entry.title: The Twisted Life of Clippy
entry.summary: The blank screen was already intimidating enough. Then, out of nowhere, an incorporeal know-it-all popped up to make us feel even worse about the novel notion of word processing in the mid-’90s.

entry.title: The Unravelling of an Expert on Serial Killers
entry.summary: A brother and a sister are standing on the balcony of a sixth-floor apartment in Monte Carlo. It’s the nineteen-seventies, in May, the afternoon of the Grand Prix.

entry.title: This 1998 Advice From Ursula K. Le Guin About Gender-Neutral Language Is Still Relevant.
entry.summary: Le Guin discusses her regrets about not using gender-neutral language. “English has a truly ungendered pronoun only in the plural.

entry.title: Title: IKEA Uses Slave Labour of Prisoners
entry.summary: Why have you decided to protest in this IKEA in Prague? I was outraged by the report on IKEA’s ten year long cooperation with Belarusian companies and its use of unpaid forced labour in Belarusian penal colonies, including the labour of political prisoners and of prisoners of conscience.

entry.title: To better address the needs of older populations, researchers and media should stop fussing over aging
entry.summary: The world seems to be obsessed with aging. The media is plagued with articles about the costs of growing older, the cure to aging and secrets to aging successfully.

entry.title: UN biodiversity conference: what does living in harmony with nature look like?
entry.summary: The 196 countries meeting for the UN Convention on Biological Diversity conference (COP15) in Montreal, Canada, are negotiating a new set of targets for reversing the loss of Earth’s biodiversity.

entry.title: Want to Retain Information Better? Try This Popular, 70-Year-Old Note-Taking Method
entry.summary: At a conference, in a conference room, or in a classroom, this method is proven and powerful. Link

entry.title: What Comes After Ambition?
entry.summary: Something’s been happening with the ambitious women in my life. A friend who used to be focused on climbing the corporate ladder in her marketing job—while dabbling in a series of side hustles—is trying to figure out how to backpedal.

entry.title: What Makes a Cult a Cult?
entry.summary: Male cult leaders sometimes claim droit du seigneur over female followers or use physical violence to sexually exploit them.

entry.title: What we mean when we say ‘sovereignty was never ceded’
entry.summary: In discussing the Uluru Statement from the Heart, I will be doing something that, as a lawyer, is perhaps not best practice: I am not going to define my terms or confine my comments to the law.

entry.title: Where to Start
entry.summary: Following on from Thriving on the Technical Leadership Path, this post suggests some things I have learned and want to share with others interested in supporting or cultivating a long-term career as an engineer, specifically those working on strategy and technical leadership.

entry.title: Wild-life: anarchy, ecology, and ethics
entry.summary: Anarchism implies and incorporates an ecological attitude towards nature. Link

entry.title: You’re Not So Bad: The Case Against Self Improvement
entry.summary: View Original Link

entry.title: ‘It’s Not Just Burnout:’ How Grind Culture Fails Women
entry.summary: View Original Link

entry.title: “My Brother Is So Far Gone”: How Male Influencers Turned The Men In These People’s Lives Toxic
entry.summary: A beloved brother who suddenly supports violence against women, a dad who minored in women’s studies and now wants to murder “effeminate” men, a boyfriend who starts making misogynistic jokes.

entry.title: Digital Justice Lab
entry.summary: We engage and collaborate with communities through public knowledge campaigns and projects. We strive to produce content that is accessible and relatable to the larger public.

entry.title: How to Take Notes While Reading
entry.summary: Note-taking is an incredibly powerful tool for learning. Notes extend your memories. I’ve explained before how writing can be seen as an external enhancement of your brain, allowing you to think more complicated thoughts and solve harder problems.

entry.title: Issuepedia
entry.summary: Issuepedia is on a deep-dive mission into the facts behind public discourse, and the deceptions that power players use to engineer public sentiment counter to those facts.

entry.title: Ministry of Ideas
entry.summary: Climate change and war have flung millions of people on the move, who often seek safe harbor in the very countries responsible for their displacement.

entry.title: Tech Colonialism Today
entry.summary: We are here today to consider whether we, as anthropologists and research practitioners, may be part of industries that are colonial.

entry.title: Delivering on an architecture strategy
entry.summary: Software companies always have an unended stream of product features for engineers to work on. One of the most important responsibilities of a technology leader is to carve out space so that engineers can also perform the technical work which keeps the architectural foundations of a product healthy.

entry.title: Effective Mental Models for Code and Systems
entry.summary: As always, I’m greatly indebted to Fred Hebert for reviewing a draft of this post and offering some invaluable insights.

entry.title: Permacomputing Update 2021
entry.summary: It is now more than a year since I wrote my “early notes” about Permacomputing. At that time, I was not yet aware of anyone else having similar ideas, so I’ve now decided to write an update that connects my ideas with the existing discussions and activities.

entry.title: Run less software
entry.summary: It’s hard to win a battle you don’t realise you’re in. It’s even harder if you don’t know all of the armies on the field, their strategies and weapons, or even who’s a friend and who’s a foe.

entry.title: Systems that defy detailed understanding
entry.summary: Last week, I wrote about the mindset that computer systems can be understood, and behaviors can be explained, if we’re willing to dig deep enough into the stack of abstractions our software is built atop.

entry.title: Restricting SSH Sessions for CI CD with GNU Rush
entry.summary: One method for deployment that addresses these requirements when deploying to remote *nix systems is SSH coupled with rsync. The only gap is that typically, even if you use SSH as a transport (encryption, authentication) you by default provide the deployment service account with a full shell.

entry.title: The Engineering Anthology
entry.summary: %% Begin Waypoint %% %% End Waypoint %%

entry.title: Cytoscape.js
entry.summary: Graph theory (network) library for visualisation and analysis. Website

entry.title: Flask Documentation
entry.summary: Website Comprehensive guide to Flask, that includes common patterns, how Flask works, and how to get started with common components, like SQL Lite.

entry.title: Python Patterns
entry.summary: Website Brandon Rhodes, evolving guide to design patterns in the Python programming language. The site allows him to collect his ideas about Python and Design Patterns all in one place.

entry.title: Refactoring Guru
entry.summary: Website Provides high-level and implementation guides for common design patterns and refactoring.

entry.title: Dopely
entry.summary: A bunch of helpful colour tools like pallet, tone, tint and shade generators. Also has digital colour tools for checking contrast and colour blindcheckers.

entry.title: Temporal
entry.summary: An open source programming model that can be used to provide in depth tracing using the concept of “business logic as code”.

entry.title: Distributed Press
entry.summary: An open source publishing tool for the World Wide Web and [[Distributed Web]]. It automates publishing to the world wide web, and seeds to IPFS and Holepunch.

entry.title: tools
entry.summary: %% Begin Waypoint %% cytoscapejs distributed-press dopely npm-ses temporal %% End Waypoint %%

entry.title: npm - SES
entry.summary: SES is hardened JavaScript. SES stands for fearless cooperation. This package is a SES shim for JavaScript features proposed to ECMA TC39.

entry.title: Languages that implement object capabilities
entry.summary: The following programming languages implement an [[object-capability-model|Object-capability model]]: Act 1 (1981) Eden (1985) Emerald (1987) Trusty Scheme (1992) W7 (1995) Joule (1996) Original-E (1997) Oz-E (2005) Joe-E (2005) CaPerl (2006) Emily (2006) Caja (2007–2021) Monte (2008–present) Pony (2014–present) Wyvern (2012–present) Newspeak (2007–present) Hacklang (2021-present) Rholang (2018-present)

entry.title: Object-capability model
entry.summary: The object-capability model (also seen written as “ocap”) is a [[object-capability security]] concept that describes a computer security model. In ocap a capability describes a transferable right to perform one or more operations on a given object.

entry.title: Gold Mafia - Episode 1 - The Laundry Service
entry.summary: Undercover reporters pose as gangsters with up to a billion dollars of black money that need to be cleaned. They gain remarkable access to members of Africa’s Gold Mafia and film closed door meetings with crime bosses.

entry.title: Tracking database changes in Flask Sqlalchemy for audit logs
entry.summary: If you ever come across a situation where you need to track the changes in the database for auditing purposes or to make the history of all changes in the database.

entry.title: OAuth 2.0 Dynamic Client Registration Protocol
entry.summary: This specification defines mechanisms for dynamically registering OAuth 2.0 clients with authorization servers. Registration requests send a set of desired client metadata values to the authorization server.

entry.title: Police are not primarily crime fighters, according to the data
entry.summary: A new report adds to a growing line of research showing that police departments don’t solve serious or violent crimes with any regularity, and in fact, spend very little time on crime control, in contrast to popular narratives.

entry.title: Archive Stories
entry.summary: Archive Stories is a website about how to work with creative and non-traditional archives. We wanted to create a space for conversations about archiving beyond institutional archives, to think through the possibilities that open up when we imagine the archive as expansive and as encompassing everything around us.

entry.title: Intercropping Examples
entry.summary: The following table was compiled on companion planting which could be seen as similar to intercropping: Crop Intercrop With Asparagus Tomato, Parsley, Basil Bush Beans Potato, Cucumber, Corn, Strawberry, Celery, Summer Savory Pole Beans Corn, Summer Savory, Radish Cabbage Family Aromatic Herbs, Celery, Beets, Onion Family, Chamomile, Spinach, Chard Carrots Radishes, Lettuce, Rosemary, Onion Family, Sage, Tomato Celery Onion, Cabbage Families, Tomato, Bush Beans, Nasturtium Corn Potato, Beans, Pumpkins, Cucumber, Squash Eggplant Beans, Marigold Lettuce Carrots, Radish, Strawberry, Cucumber Onion Family Beets, Carrots, Lettuce, Cabbage Family, Summer Savory Parsley Tomato, Asparagus Potato Beans, Corn, Cabbage Family, Marigolds, Horseradish Pumpkins Beans, Corn, Marigold Radish Carrots, Nasturtium, Lettuce, Cucumber Spinach Strawberry, Beans Squash Nasturtium, Corn, Marigold Tomato Onion Family, Nasturtium, Marigold, Asparagus, Carrots, Parsley, Cucumber Turnip Aromatic Herbs, Celery, Beets, Onion Family, Chamomile, Spinach, Chard

entry.title: eutrophication
entry.summary: The process by which an entire body of water, or parts of it become enriched with minerals and nutrients, particularly nitrogen and phosphorus.

entry.title: Polyculture Practices
entry.summary: While there are no hard or fast rules to using a [[notes/polyculture|Polyculture]] there are common practices that can be implemented depending on the plants, spatial distribution and time spent cohabiting.

entry.title: polyculture
entry.summary: A polyculture is the practice of growing more than one crop or species in the same space at the same time.

entry.title: bookmarks
entry.summary: %% Begin Waypoint %% [[5-types-of-threat-how-those-who-want-to-divide-us-use-language-to-stoke-violence]] [[17-forbidden-places-you-cant-visit]] [[a-dangerous-pesticide-isnt-being-monitored-in-key-bird-of-prey-populations-were-shedding-light-on-that-gap]] [[a-massachusetts-bill-could-allow-prisoners-to-swap-their-organs-for-their-freedom]] [[a-racially-biased-scoring-system-helps-pick-who-receives-housing]] [[a-small-mistake-does-not-a-complex-systems-failure-make]] [[a-story-of-legends-families-and-capitalism-a-candid-history-of-the-christmas-tree]] [[abolition-ecology-and-afrofuturism-some-thoughts]] [[adjusting-the-intensity-of-farming-can-help-address-climate-change]] [[against-a-liberal-abolitionism]] [[against-self-work-a-critique-of-care-as-a-form-of-labor]] [[all-my-takeout-has-delivered-a-mountain-of-trash-so-i-asked-experts-how-to-minimize-it]] [[anarchism-and-cryptocurrency]] [[andrew-tate-research-has-long-shown-how-feminist-progress-is-always-followed-by-a-misogynistic-backlash]] [[anti-racism-and-anti-colonialism-an-open-letter-to-my-black-kin]] [[april-king-refresh-vs.

entry.title: Using Pytest with Flask
entry.summary: What to test? Unit tests should focus on testing a small unit of code in isolation: Database models Utility functions Functional tests should focus on how the view functions operate:

entry.title: NASA's ADLR Framework for Lessons Learned
entry.summary: Example: NASA Lesson Number 5756 The ADLR framework is broken up into four parts: Abstract: a two or three sentence summary of what happened.

entry.title: anarchic illustrations
entry.summary: Drawings, prints and stickers promoting the abolition of traditional forms of government and institutions. All my anarchic themed illustrations are available as high-res downloads, available under CC by 4.

entry.title: All Crustaceans Really Are Beautiful
Sauerkraut Recipe
entry.summary: Sauerkraut recipe for Week 26, 2023. Ingredients 2453g cabbage 674g carrot 252g apple 450g celeriac (forgot to weigh it, so this is a guess) 1 packet of sauerkraut seasoning 0.

entry.title: Ghost Work
entry.summary: “A startling exposé of the invisible human workforce that powers the web–and how to bring it out of the shadows. Hidden beneath the surface of the internet, a new, stark reality is looming–one that cuts to the very heart of our endless debates about the impact of AI.

entry.title: Erlaubnis Project Diagrams
entry.summary: These diagrams can also be found on Github. --- title: Erlaubnis Authorization State Diagram --- stateDiagram-v2 [*] -- Request Request -- Verifing: Exchange Client Params Verifing -- Waiting_Approval: Grant Valid Verifing -- Unauthorised: Grant Invalid Waiting_Approval -- Authorised: Grant Approved Waiting_Approval -- Unauthorised: Grant Denied Waiting_Approval -- Timed_Out: Grant Unserviced Authorised -- Revoked: Grant Revoked Revoked -- [*] Timed_Out -- [*] Unauthorised -- [*] Authorised -- [*]--- title: Erlaubnis Entity Relationship Diagram --- erDiagram USER { string id string username string password datetime created_at datetime updated_at } ROLE { integer id string name string description } CLIENT { string id string client_name string token_endpoint_auth_method array grant_types } METADATA { string logo_uri array contacts string policy_uri string tos_uri string client_uri } CONFIGURATION { integer version string jwks string jwks_uri string scope } REGISTRATION_RECORD { int id foreign_key user_id foreign_key client_id datetime installation_time } INSTALLATION_RECORD { int id foreign_key user_id foreign_key client_id foreign_key configuration datetime installation_time } STATE_RECORD { foreign_key user_id foreign_key client string state } USER ||--o{ ROLE : has USER ||--o{ CLIENT : registers REGISTRATION_RECORD ||--o{ CLIENT : generates INSTALLATION_RECORD ||--o{ CLIENT : generates STATE_RECORD ||--o{ CLIENT : generates USER ||--o{ CLIENT : installs CLIENT ||--o{ CONFIGURATION : contains CLIENT ||--||METADATA : contains

entry.title: neocolonialism
entry.summary: Intrusion of foreign economic domination, it can include military and economic intervention in the form of structural adjustment programs. Neocolonialism is seem in states that have already achieved independence from colonial rule.

entry.title: structural adjustment programs
entry.summary: Structural adjustment programs are a [[notes/neocolonialism|neocolonist]] tool that sets out a set of economic reforms that a country must adhere to secure a loan from the International Monetary Fund and/or the World Bank.

entry.title: cash crops
entry.summary: A crop that is grown to sell for profit rather than for the benefit of the farmer. Used to distinguish between marketed crops from subsistence crops in subsistence agriculture.

entry.title: Expect Resistance: A Crimethink Field Manual
entry.summary: Expect Resistance is not one but three books, each of which may be read as a complete work unto itself. The first book, printed in standard black ink, continues the inquiry into modern life and its discontents begun in Days of War, Nights of Love.

entry.title: Consensus and Humming in the IETF
entry.summary: This post summarises the key points of RFC 7282 an informational RFC (Request for Comment) published in 2014. This post is?

entry.title: E2E Fear Campaign -- ACCCE & Police
entry.summary: Post on Twitter Post on Mastodon 1/14 The ACCCE & police are currently running a fear campaign about end to end encryption centering their arguments this time on concerns about sextortion of teenagers.

entry.title: Embodiment
entry.summary: The principles of embodiment look at diverse experiences and relationships as human beings embodying multiple identities and realities in “disembodied” online spaces.

entry.title: Feminist Principles of the Internet
entry.summary: The Feminist Principles of the Internet are a series of statements that offer a gender and sexual rights lens on critical Internet-related rights.

entry.title: Freedom is not Given its Taken
surveillance capitalism
entry.summary: Surveillance capitalism is a type of capitalism market form, and a [[technocratic]]-oriented phenomenon that centres on the commodification of personal data by corporations.

entry.title: technocratic
entry.summary: Technocracy is a form of government in which the decision-makers are selected based on their expertise in a given area of responsibility, particularly with regard to scientific or technical knowledge.

entry.title: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
entry.summary: The challenges to humanity posed by the digital future, the first detailed examination of the unprecedented form of power called ‘surveillance capitalism,’ and the quest by powerful corporations to predict and control us.

entry.title: the state
entry.summary: The “state” is a centralised political organisation that imposes and enforces rules over a population within a territory. [!warning]

entry.title: Work in Progress
entry.summary: Work that is still in progress and being worked on. Freedom is not Given its TakenCheck it out on Ko-Fi →

entry.title: Autobase
entry.summary: An experimental module that’s used to rebase (follow?) multiple casually linked [[hypercore|Hypercore]] into a single, linearized Hypercore for multi-user collaboration.

entry.title: BTree
entry.summary: A self-balancing tree data structure that is used to store data in a sorted and searchable manner.

entry.title: DHT
entry.summary: A decentralized data structure that is used to store and retrieve data over a network. The HyperDHT is used to facilitate the discovery of peers and to enable connections between them.

entry.title: DID
entry.summary: Decentralized identifier (DIDs) identifies any subject (e.g., a person, organization, thing, data model, abstract entity, etc.) that the controller of the DID decides that it identifies.

entry.title: Holepunch
entry.summary: Holepunch is a company and suite of tools that “equips developers with a powerful suite of independent components to effortlessly construct peer-to-peer applications.

entry.title: Hyperbee
entry.summary: An append-only [[b-tree|B-Tree]] running on Hypercore. It provides a key-value store API with mechanisms that can be used for: - Inserting and retrieving key-value pairs; - Atomic batch insertion; - Creation of sorted iterators.

entry.title: Hypercore
entry.summary: A distributed, peer to peer, append-only log that can be used to create fast scalable applications without a backend.

entry.title: HyperDHT
entry.summary: A [[dht|DHT]] powering [[Hyperswarm]], each server is bound to a unique key pair, where the client connects to the server using the servers public key.

entry.title: Hyperdrive
entry.summary: A real-time distributed file system.

entry.title: Hyperswarm
entry.summary: A high-level API for finding and connecting to peers who are interested in a “topic”.

entry.title: IPFS
entry.summary: InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) is a protocol, hypermedia and file sharing peer-to-peer network for storing and sharing data to a distributed file system.

entry.title: Setting Up Distributed Press on Digital Ocean
entry.summary: [!info] Environment Metadata Ubuntu 22.10 x64 Distributed Press v1.2.1 About Distributed Press Distributed Press is an open source publishing tool for the World Wide Web and Distributed Web.

entry.title: The Inner Sanctum
entry.summary: The Inner Sanctum (2022), gouache, watercolor, and ink on paper, 100 x 70 centimeters, Colossal

entry.title: The Offering
entry.summary: The Offering, gouache painting by Bill Mayer, Bill Mayer’s Personal Website

entry.title: autumn pumpkins
Character Studies
entry.summary: Little illustrations and spreads that have been created to explore the traits of characters, featuring my favourites from television shows, movies and my imagination.

entry.title: Vegetable Portraits
entry.summary: Portraits of my favourite and seasonal vegetables created using a variety of artistic mediums, they act as consistent theme that records changes in art style.

entry.title: reproductive labor
entry.summary: Reproductive labor was first brought into the discourse during the wages for housework campaign during the 1970s (discussed in Revolution at Point Zero).

entry.title: 👑 Catherine the Great - A Tiny Character Study
Aquarium Algae Scrubber
entry.summary: An algae scrubber is a water filtering device thats used to grow algae, which in the process, filters undesirable chemicals like nitrate, phosphate, nitrite, ammonia, ammonium from the water.

entry.title: Ars moriendi
entry.summary: Ars moriendi, woodblock 7 of 11, Circa 1460, illustrator unknown, en wikipedia. Source The Ars Moriendi (The Art of Dying) is a Mediaeval Christian death manual that appeared around the middle of the fifteenth century.

entry.title: Coaster for Salut
entry.summary: Coaster for Salute, gouache painting by @rowan_sugar, Coaster 3/6 for the Salut!, 2023

entry.title: Lago d'Averno
entry.summary: Lago d’Averno, gouache painting by Jacob Philipp Hackert, 1794.

entry.title: The history of witches and wizards
entry.summary: The history of witches and wizards: giving a true account of all their tryals in England, Scotland, Swedeland, France, and New England; with their confession and condemnation, circa 1720, Collected from Bishop Hall, Bishop Morton, Sir Matthew Hale, etc.

entry.title: Theatrum Chemicum Britannicum
entry.summary: Theatrum Chemicum Britannicum, woodblock, circa 1652, Elias Ashmole, The Public Domain Review

entry.title: Witches dancing in a circle with the devil
entry.summary: The history of witches and wizards: giving a true account of all their tryals in England, Scotland, Swedeland, France, and New England; with their confession and condemnation, circa 1720, Collected from Bishop Hall, Bishop Morton, Sir Matthew Hale, etc.

entry.title: Witches flying on broomsticks
entry.summary: The history of witches and wizards: giving a true account of all their tryals in England, Scotland, Swedeland, France, and New England; with their confession and condemnation, circa 1720, Collected from Bishop Hall, Bishop Morton, Sir Matthew Hale, etc.

entry.title: woodblock
entry.summary: A technique originally developed in China as a way of printing on textiles and later on paper, the oldest surviving fragments to survive date back to the Han dynasty (before 220).

entry.title: 100 days of gouache
entry.summary: The concept of a 100 days project is you commit to doing something consistently for 100 days straight. For a non sequential 100 days I created a [[notes/gouache|gouache]] painting using 100 Days of Drawing by Jennifer Lewis.

entry.title: gouache
entry.summary: Gouache is a water based medium made of pigment, water and a binding agent, normally gum arabic or dextrin. Gouache is similar to watercolour in that it can be re-hydrated and it infuses with the paper.

entry.title: The Feminist and the Sex Offender
entry.summary: In the era of Me Too and mass incarceration, The Feminist and the Sex Offender makes a powerful feminist case for accountability without punishment and sexual safety and pleasure without injustice.

entry.title: the art studio
entry.summary: If you’ve been enjoying my work and finding it entertaining. Your support means the world to me, and I can’t thank you enough for being a part of this journey.

entry.title: Anarchic Agreements, a Field Guide to Collective Organising
entry.summary: A new world is possible and not just in our hearts. Anarchic Agreements is a quintessential field guide for the revolution, answering the practical questions often left out of works of political theory and philosophy.

entry.title: the antilibrary
entry.summary: a scholar conscious of the power of [their] antilibrary is not concerned with treating knowledge as a property to possess or consume; rather […] how much you don’t know—and how to find out that information when you need it.

entry.title: errbufferoverfl
entry.summary: The easiest way to pronounce it is “error buffer overflow”… like the security vulnerability.

entry.title: Wadawurrung and Dja Dja Wurrung People
entry.summary: Wadawurrung Country Wadawurrung country encompasses the area from the Great Dividing Range of Ballarat, the coast from the Werribee River to Mangowak (Airey’s Inlet), including Djilang (Geelong), Ballaarat (Ballarat), Torquay, Jan Jook, the Bellarine Peninsula and Surf Coast.

entry.title: Do What Makes You Happy
Fuck Capitalism
Lost in Work Escaping Capitalism
entry.summary: ‘Work hard, get paid.’ It’s simple. Self-evident. But it’s also a lie—at least for most of us. For young people today, the old assumptions are crumbling; hard work in school no longer guarantees a secure, well-paying job in the future.

entry.title: Revolution at Point Zero
entry.summary: Written between 1974 and 2016, Revolution at Point Zero collects four decades of research and theorising on the nature of housework, social reproduction, and women’s struggles on this terrain—to escape it, to better its conditions, to reconstruct it in ways that provide an alternative to capitalist relations.

entry.title: Collapsology: Why your biggest threat isn’t exposed RDP
entry.summary: In September 2021, I presented a conference talk entitled “Collapsology: Why Exposed RDP Isn’t Your Biggest Threat” where I gave an overview of how to identify cultural threats in a rapidly evolving business landscape, and how we can use modern-day tools to identify these threats before they result in security culture collapse.

entry.title: The Big Cloud Security Debate: Hackers vs Security Engineers
entry.summary: A debate organised by ANZ, Contino and Meetup Madness where 4 hackers vs 4 security experts engaging in a fiery debate to determine all the ways they can hack and defend cloud environments.

entry.title: A Brief History of Tamper Evident Locks
entry.summary: A talk that discussed common “canary locks” or locks with tamper evident mechanisms through the ages, Buffy trawled through 11,335,427 patents to identify several high and low profile locks and their known, or speculated bypass techniques.

entry.title: Snake Charming for Beginners
entry.summary: Snake-charming is an age-old practice of hypnotizing snakes by playing and waving a murli - in the modern day this practice looks much different, equipped with an Integrated Development Environment (IDE), a clackity keyboard and a trusty guide we’ll be taking you through how you can effectively charm Python 3.

entry.title: Linux Users Victoria Main Meeting Kali Linux
entry.summary: Come together in ritual to learn about KALI LINUX! A Debian based Penetration Testing and Ethical Hacking distribution, with over 600 pre-installed packages.

entry.title: Linux Users Victoria Main Meeting Kali Linux
entry.summary: Come together in ritual to learn about KALI LINUX! A Debian based Penetration Testing and Ethical Hacking distribution, with over 600 pre-installed packages.

entry.title: Agloe: What the Map Makers of the 1930s can Teach us About Protecting our Data in 2018
entry.summary: What does the little town of Agloe, Colchester, NY have in common with modern day data protection? Why when I look for directions to Agloe, Colchester, NY do I only get a partial match?

entry.title: Building your own offline file-sharing and communication system for the inevitable downfall of the Internet
entry.summary: Have you ever considered a world where the Internet in all it’s grandeur and tyre-fire-ness has finally imploded. Well after living for 8 months without the Internet I did and it looked a little like this.
